lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O, look, Titinius, look, the villains fly!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Myself have to mine own turn’d enemy:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This ensign here of mine was turning back;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I slew the coward, and did take it from him.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: O Cassius, Brutus gave the word too early;

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Who, having some advantage on Octavius,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Took it too eagerly: his soldiers fell to spoil,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Whilst we by Antony are all enclosed.

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Fly further off, my lord, fly further off;

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Mark Antony is in your tents, my lord:

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Fly, therefore, noble Cassius, fly far off.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This hill is far enough. Look, look, Titinius;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Are those my tents where I perceive the fire?

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: They are, my lord.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Titinius, if thou lovest me,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Mount thou my horse, and hide thy spurs in him,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Till he have brought thee up to yonder troops,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And here again; that I may rest assured

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Whether yond troops are friend or enemy.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: I will be here again, even with a thought.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Go, Pindarus, get higher on that hill;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: My sight was ever thick; regard Titinius,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And tell me what thou notest about the field. Pindarus ascends the hill.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This day I breathed first: time is come round,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And where I did begin, there shall I end;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: My life is run his compass. Sirrah, what news?

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Above. O my lord!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What news?

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Above. Titinius is enclosed round about

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: With horsemen, that make to him on the spur;

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Yet he spurs on. Now they are almost on him.

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Now, Titinius! Now some light. O, he lights too.

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: He’s ta’en. And, hark! they shout for joy.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Come down, behold no more.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O, coward that I am, to live so long,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To see my best friend ta’en before my face!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Come hither, sirrah:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In Parthia did I take thee prisoner;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And then I swore thee, saving of thy life,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That whatsoever I did bid thee do,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Thou shouldst attempt it. Come now, keep thine oath;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Now be a freeman: and with this good sword,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That ran through Caesar’s bowels, search this bosom.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Stand not to answer: here, take thou the hilts;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And, when my face is cover’d, as ’tis now,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Guide thou the sword. Caesar, thou art revenged,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Even with the sword that kill’d thee.

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: So, I am free; yet would not so have been,

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Durst I have done my will. O Cassius,

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Far from this country Pindarus shall run,

lines.speaker: PINDARUS
lines.text: Where never Roman shall take note of him.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: It is but change, Titinius; for Octavius

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Is overthrown by noble Brutus’ power,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: As Cassius’ legions are by Antony.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: These tidings will well comfort Cassius.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Where did you leave him?

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: All disconsolate,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: With Pindarus his bondman, on this hill.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Is not that he that lies upon the ground?

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: He lies not like the living. O my heart!

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Is not that he?

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: No, this was he, Messala,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: But Cassius is no more. O setting sun,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: As in thy red rays thou dost sink to-night,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: So in his red blood Cassius’ day is set;

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: The sun of Rome is set! Our day is gone;

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Clouds, dews, and dangers come; our deeds are done!

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Mistrust of my success hath done this deed.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Mistrust of good success hath done this deed.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: O hateful error, melancholy’s child,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Why dost thou show to the apt thoughts of men

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: The things that are not? O error, soon conceived,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Thou never comest unto a happy birth,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: But kill’st the mother that engender’d thee!

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: What, Pindarus! where art thou, Pindarus?

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Seek him, Titinius, whilst I go to meet

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: The noble Brutus, thrusting this report

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Into his ears; I may say, thrusting it;

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: For piercing steel and darts envenomed

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Shall be as welcome to the ears of Brutus

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: As tidings of this sight.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Hie you, Messala,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: And I will seek for Pindarus the while.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Why didst thou send me forth, brave Cassius?

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Did I not meet thy friends? and did not they

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Put on my brows this wreath of victory,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: And bid me give it thee? Didst thou not hear their shouts?

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Alas, thou hast misconstrued everything!

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: But, hold thee, take this garland on thy brow;

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Thy Brutus bid me give it thee, and I

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Will do his bidding. Brutus, come apace,

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: And see how I regarded Caius Cassius.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: By your leave, gods:—this is a Roman’s part

lines.speaker: TITINIUS
lines.text: Come, Cassius’ sword, and find Titinius’ heart.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Where, where, Messala, doth his body lie?

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Lo, yonder, and Titinius mourning it.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Titinius’ face is upward.

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: He is slain.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O Julius Caesar, thou art mighty yet!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thy spirit walks abroad, and turns our swords

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: In our own proper entrails. Low alarums.

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: Brave Titinius!

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: Look, whether he have not crown’d dead Cassius!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Are yet two Romans living such as these?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The last of all the Romans, fare thee well!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It is impossible that ever Rome

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Should breed thy fellow. Friends, I owe more tears

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To this dead man than you shall see me pay.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I shall find time, Cassius, I shall find time.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come, therefore, and to Thasos send his body:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: His funerals shall not be in our camp,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lest it discomfort us. Lucilius, come;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And come, young Cato; let us to the field.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Labeo and Flavius, set our battles on:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: ’Tis three o’clock; and, Romans, yet ere night

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: We shall try fortune in a second fight.

stage_directions.text: Alarum. Reenter Messala, with Brutus, young Cato, Strato, Volumnius, and Lucilius.

glosses.definition: Cassius's own troops are fleeing — his left wing has broken
standard-bearer; here, the man carrying the legionary standard
stopped to loot — a classical tactical error; Brutus's men were plundering Octavius's camp instead of pressing the attack
Cassius halts his retreat — the battlefield vantage from which he mistakes Titinius's capture for death
my eyesight has always been poor — the visual error that causes his suicide
today is my birthday (echoing 5.1); now I die on the day I was born
my life has run its full circle
the Parthian Empire east of Rome (modern Iran); Cassius campaigned there under Crassus in 53 BCE, where Pindarus became his prisoner
the very sword Cassius used to kill Caesar — now turned upon himself; a perfect Roman poetic justice
Caesar is avenged — Cassius acknowledges the moral symmetry of his death
turn of fortune; reverse — Messala's mistaken good news
Cassius is dead (announced by Titinius)
the evening sun — classical image for a great man's death; Julius Caesar was called the sun of Rome; now so is Cassius
Rome's great day has ended — Titinius's lament for Cassius extends the solar metaphor to the Republic itself

glosses.phrase: Mistrust of my success hath done this deed
glosses.definition: distrust in my victory caused this — Cassius's misreading of Titinius's fate killed him
melancholy's offspring — error as the child of a morbid temperament
placed a victory-wreath on Cassius's head — Titinius's last service before killing himself
the Roman role — Stoic suicide as performance of Roman virtue
the ghost roams free — Caesar's revenge is spreading
in our very own guts — we are killing ourselves; the suicides of 5.3 continue in 5.5
Brutus's epitaph for Cassius — the last true Roman of the old school; an elegy for the Republic itself. The play argues through Brutus that the Republic died with Cassius
Greek island in the north Aegean, not far from Philippi; Brutus sends Cassius's body there for burial
dishearten our troops (not modern 'discomfort')

annotations.title: Cassius's suicide: the sword that killed Caesar turned inward
annotations.body:
Cassius's suicide is a model of Roman poetic justice. He instructs his freedman Pindarus to guide 'this good sword, / That ran through Caesar's bowels' into his own bosom. The same blade that opened Caesar's body in 3.1 now opens Cassius's. Shakespeare makes the moral symmetry explicit: 'Caesar, thou art revenged, / Even with the sword that kill'd thee.'

The suicide also turns on a mistake. Titinius, sent to verify whether the distant troops are friendly or enemy, is in fact greeted by Brutus's victorious forces — but Pindarus, watching from the hilltop with his master's poor eyesight in the equation, reports that Titinius has been captured. Cassius, on the bad news, kills himself. Titinius returns to find Cassius dead and kills himself in grief. The play's most politically consequential suicide is caused by a misreading.

Messala's moralising response — 'O hateful error, melancholy's child, / Why dost thou show to the apt thoughts of men / The things that are not?' — turns the incident into an emblem of melancholy's power to generate false perception and fatal action. Shakespeare is showing, one more time, the theme that runs from Brutus's soliloquy through Calpurnia's dream-interpretation to this scene: political actors consistently kill on the basis of misread signs. The theme is not coincidence; it is the play's argument.

annotations.title: 'The last of all the Romans': Brutus's elegy for the Republic
annotations.body:
Brutus's epitaph for Cassius — 'The last of all the Romans, fare thee well! / It is impossible that ever Rome / Should breed thy fellow' — is the play's first explicit statement that the Republic has effectively ended. Brutus is not saying that Cassius was the last human Roman; he is saying that the old republican type, of which Cassius was the best example, cannot be bred again. Rome has changed. The institutions that produced Cassius are gone.

The line is also an elegy for the speaker. Brutus is himself a Roman of that type; if Cassius was the last, Brutus is about to be the second-to-last. The scene is structurally the death of the old order in two persons: Cassius first, Brutus to come. Antony's 5.5 epitaph — 'This was the noblest Roman of them all' — will complete the arc by applying the same language to Brutus. Both Romans are dead; the new Rome — Octavius's Rome, the coming empire — has no place for their kind.

trials.prompt: Why does Cassius instruct Pindarus to use 'the sword / That ran through Caesar's bowels'?
options: The same blade that killed Caesar now kills Cassius — Roman poetic justice made physical; Shakespeare explicitly frames the suicide as Caesar's revenge via Cassius's own hand
A genuine defeat at that moment

Pindarus's misreading of distant friendly troops as enemy — Cassius kills himself on false information; Messala's 'hateful error, melancholy's child' turns the incident into an emblem of misreading-as-political-cause, a pattern the play traces from 2.1 through the whole action

trials.prompt: Brutus's 'The last of all the Romans' is:
options: Literal — no more Romans will be born

An elegy for the republican type — the institutional conditions that produced Cassius are gone; Rome has changed, and the man Cassius was cannot be bred again; the line also foreshadows Brutus's own death and the Republic's end
